Create a retrieval-oriented summary for this section of a document.
Focus on what questions this section can answer: topics, entities, dates,
definitions, procedures, claims, tables, figures, metrics, and evidence types.
Do not invent facts. Return only the summary text.
This is text window 1 of 3 for the section.

Section metadata:
{
  "node_id": "0015",
  "title": "Community Safety",
  "page_index": 23,
  "page_range": "23-24"
}

Section text:
Community Safety
In the 1990s video games moved online from offline,
and games such as Massively Multiplayer Online
Role-Playing Games (MMORPGs) and First-Person
Shooters (FPS), laid the platform for community
interaction. Moderation was largely left to the
players and in-game administrators, who monitored
a few instances of cheating and toxicity.
However, as the scale of these games grew, so
did the instances of misconduct and by the late
1990s community managers were hired to manage
player behaviour and enforce rules. By the 2000s
the emergence of social games and mobile gaming
resulted in a sharp increase in rule-breaking,
harassment, and other toxic behaviours and studios
were forced to implement advanced safety systems
such as in-game reporting tools and dedicated
moderation teams. By the end of the decade, the
moderation function began to be outsourced to
Business Process Outsourcing (BPO) providers, which
meant only companies with large user bases could
afford it.
As online games started to become more socially
integrated and community-oriented, violations
such as bullying, child safety, cheating, and fraud
became commonplace, which resulted in the
adoption of advanced automated moderation
tools which were often powered by artificial
intelligence (AI).
The emergence of large gaming platforms with
millions of players led to the formation of dedicated
Player Experience Teams, who act as trust and safety
gatekeepers by handling player complaints and
enforcing community guidelines. They use AI and
machine learning (ML) to analyse large amounts
of data, identify issues proactively, recognize
patterns of disruptive behaviour in real time, and
flag offenders or take automated actions to mitigate
harm. Interestingly, these teams also promote
prosocial behaviour by rewarding positive acts.
With more games having live multiplayer modes
with multimodal inputs, gaming companies are
developing automated detection and actioning
solutions that minimise latency to offer real-
time or near real-time response to violations.
These solutions use technologies such as natural
language processing (text), image recognition
(visuals), and speech-to-text (audio) to create
unified systems that regulate not only in-game
events but also those taking place in related off-
platform communities.
As online games started to become more
socially integrated and community-
oriented, violations such as bullying,
child safety, cheating, and fraud
became commonplace, which resulted
in the adoption of advanced automated
moderation tools which were often
powered by artificial intelligence (AI).

message.content:
**Topics:** Answers questions regarding the historical evolution of video game community safety, the transition from manual to AI-driven moderation, the impact of social and mobile gaming on player toxicity, and the regulatory and platform frameworks governing online gaming safety.

**Entities:** Identifies key game genres (MMORPGs, FPS), moderation models (BPO providers, Player Experience Teams), technologies (AI, ML, NLP, image recognition, speech-to-text), regulatory bodies (EU, Ofcom, U.S. Federal Government, ESRB, PEGI, ESA), and major platforms (Xbox, PlayStation, Nintendo).

**Dates:** Covers the 1990s (shift to online gaming), late 1990s (introduction of dedicated community managers), 2000s (rise of social/mobile gaming and BPO outsourcing), and references the 2025 industry report publication year.

**Definitions:** Clarifies concepts such as community safety, Player Experience Teams (trust and safety gatekeepers), automated moderation, multimodal input detection, and Business Process Outsourcing (BPO) in gaming contexts.

**Procedures:** Details the stepwise evolution of moderation practices (player-led → admin-monitored → dedicated managers → in-house teams → outsourced BPO → AI/ML automation → real-time multimodal systems) and outlines Player Experience Team workflows (complaint handling, guideline enforcement, AI-driven pattern recognition, proactive flagging, and prosocial behavior incentivization).

**Claims:** Supports assertions that increased game scale and social integration correlate with higher rates of toxicity, cheating, and fraud; that AI/ML is essential for proactive, real-time moderation across in-game and off-platform communities; and that regulatory compliance and platform-specific standards are critical for mitigating harm.

**Tables:** Contains TABLE 9, which answers questions about global and regional safety regulations (DSA, OSA, COPPA, ESRB, PEGI, ESA) and platform-specific community standards (Xbox, PlayStation, Nintendo), including governing authorities, objectives, coverage scope, and geographic applicability.

**Figures:** No figures are present in this text window.

**Metrics:** Provides qualitative scale indicators (e.g., “millions of players,” “large amounts of data,” “real-time or near real-time response”) but contains no explicit numerical KPIs or statistical data.

**Evidence Types:** Draws on historical industry evolution narratives, regulatory compliance matrices, platform policy frameworks, and industry report context (The Global State of Game Publishing and Marketing 2025).
